title: Fast Delivery: B2B Field Experiments Amid COVID-19 Outbreak and New Normal
abstract: Procuring essential products during disasters can save lives and livelihoods; nevertheless, limited research has explored this critical issue. In this paper, we collaborate with a buying company to run a unique B2B field experiment during the early COVID-19 outbreak that prompts an urgent need for personal protective equipment. We find that suppliers would deliver products faster by quoting a significantly shorter lead time when the buying company purchases products for donation rather than for resale. Similarly, suppliers quote a shorter lead time when the company provides crisis information—the number of COVID-19 cases in the region with high crisis levels—to highlight the urgent need for products. Interestingly, faster delivery does not come at the expense of the buying company. Buyers receive the products faster but also pay less. We collaborate with the same company to rerun the field experiment in the new normal and find that the buyer’s intent of donation leads to shorter lead time but not to lower wholesale price. Moreover, providing the COVID-19 information of the local community to suppliers has no impact on their decisions. Overall, our paper provides the first empirical evidence on suppliers’ lead time and wholesale price decisions during and after a disaster, thereby helping companies to deliver emergency products in a timely and efficient manner as well as designing their philanthropic and information strategies.

author.Meng Li: Bauer College of Business, University of Houston, Houston, Texas 77204
author.Shichen Zhang: Business School, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

article.1. Introduction: Over centuries, mankind has suffered from significant disasters, defined as serious disruptions to the functioning of a community that exceed its capacity to cope using its own resources.1Since the 1960s, the frequency of disasters surged tenfold by 2019,2with forecasts indicating a projected fivefold increase in the coming decades (Thomas and Kopczak 2007). Beyond economic losses, disasters give rise to humanitarian crises, leading to fatalities and famines. For instance, the recent COVID-19 pandemic affected individuals in more than 200 countries and territories, resulting in 6.4 million deaths worldwide as of July 2022. Other catastrophic disasters include the 1346 Black Death, causing 75 million to 200 million fatalities, the 1931 China floods with 42 million to 400 million fatalities, World War I and II, with more than 10 million and 70 million fatalities, respectively, and the ongoing Ukraine-Russia conflict. Delivering relief supplies quickly and efficiently to victims is an important yet challenging task. To save the lives of victims or alleviate their suffering, organizations or companies must purchase and distribute relief supplies to frontline responders and victims (Apte 2010,Ergun et al. 2010). Despite widespread acknowledgment among practitioners and scholars regarding the critical role of supply chain management in disasters, empirical research on this vital issue remains surprisingly limited. The primary challenge lies in accessing confidential business-to-business (B2B) data during the typically brief period of a disaster outbreak, compounded by its inherent noise. The existing understanding is based largely on data from normal markets without disasters, which are often more readily available. During disasters, B2B markets diverge significantly from their normal counterparts across various dimensions: end users (victims versus consumers), supply (scarce versus abundant), demand (urgent versus planned), information (scarce versus abundant), and performance measure (loss minimization versus profit maximization). Moreover, disasters might have conflicting impacts on human behavior. The disaster-as-divider hypothesis predicts that disasters motivate individuals to prioritize short-term needs over long-term relationships (Boehm 2012), prompting antisocial or even violent behaviors (Ember and Ember 1992,Prediger et al. 2014). However, the disaster-as-galvanizer hypothesis predicts that disasters motivate increased prosocial behavior by evoking empathy and emotional feelings (Vardy and Atkinson 2019). Given the fundamental differences in markets with and without disasters, the conflicting impact of disasters on human behavior, the escalating frequency of disasters, and the pivotal role of supply chain management in disaster relief, there is a critical need for empirical studies on B2B inventory sourcing and delivery during disasters. 1.1. Research Questions We study how a supplier decides on lead time and wholesale price for a business buyer facing essential item shortages in a disaster. Specifically, we study how the buyer’s intent, whether for resale or donation, influences the suppliers’ lead time and wholesale price quotations. Buying companies, like other commercial entities, can either resell relief supplies for profit or opt to donate them to victims, particularly in urgent situations where direct provision proves more effective than monetary contributions. Notably, during the COVID-19 outbreak, companies such as Facebook and Apple procured substantial quantities of medical products and donated them to individuals in need of personal protective equipment (PPE). Prior literature has found that business-to-consumer (B2C) consumers respond to business philanthropic actions (Lev et al. 2010,Hainmueller et al. 2015,Singh et al. 2019). In contrast to B2C consumers, who make decisions based on personal emotions, feelings, and experiences, B2B suppliers typically operate through a team of professionals focused on overarching goals and profits rather than individual emotions. In the realm of B2B transactions, characterized by increased complexity and rationality compared with B2C interactions, certain behaviors prevalent in B2C markets—such as gender and nationality discrimination—may not be as prominent (Cui et al. 2021,2022). We thus study whether and how B2B suppliers respond to the donation behavior of the buyer. We also investigate whether the crisis information accompanying the buyer’s order affects suppliers’ lead time and wholesale pricing. Information plays a pivotal role in effective disaster responses (Ergun et al. 2010,Ye et al. 2020). During disasters, there is an immediate need for critical supplies, but limited information about the crisis and victims’ needs poses a challenge. Insufficient details on time, location, emergency level, and the number of people requiring assistance can result in ineffective response strategies, as evidenced by significant losses because of information issues during the 2004 tsunami in Banda Aceh and the 2005 Pakistan earthquake (Apte 2010). 1.2. B2B Experiments and Results In collaboration with a buying company, we conducted a B2B field experiment during the early stages of the COVID-19 outbreak, a period marked by a surge in demand for PPE that overwhelmed the available supply. The buying company, located in a severely affected region of the United States, sought to source and either resell or donate PPE products to the local community. The company sources products viaalibaba.com(Alibaba), the largest global trading platform in the world (Alibaba 2023). This process involves sending inquiries to the suppliers about the lead time (or the delivery time, i.e., the time between order placement and the order being ready for delivery) and the wholesale price along with additional information regarding the purchase: (i) the reason for the purchase—whether it is for resale or donation—and (ii) crisis information, including the number of confirmed COVID-19 cases in the state and the state’s ranking for the disease in the United States. We compare the suppliers’ lead times—perhaps the most important measure in a disaster—for two buyer intentions (donation and resale) and two information conditions (crisis information provided or not). First, expressing donation intent leads to a quote with a significantly shorter lead time (almost 40% faster) compared with resale. This is interesting because it is optimal for suppliers to discard the information regarding the buyer’s intention, because it is not verifiable. Rather, we find that suppliers still factor in the buyer’s intention information when making the lead time quotation decisions. The donation information can signal both the immediate needs of the affected population and the buyer’s philanthropic behavior, fostering altruism and prosocial preferences in the supplier. Consequently, this leads to faster delivery, benefiting both the buyer and the affected population. Second, presenting crisis information results in a quote with a significantly shorter lead time (more than 35% faster) compared with scenarios without crisis information. This is because crisis information reveals the number of victims in the region and the severity of the crisis relative to other regions, which may prompt suppliers to become aware of the immediate needs of victims and take action to alleviate these needs. We also explore the influence of donation intent and crisis information on suppliers’ wholesale pricing. Although intuition suggests that the urgent need signaled by donation intent and crisis information might lead to a higher price for necessities, we find that suppliers quote a cheaper wholesale price when products are purchased for donation or when crisis information is provided. That is, faster delivery through donation or crisis information is not only free but potentially less costly for the buyer. This suggests that suppliers’ prosocial considerations play a dominant role in shaping lead time and wholesale price quotations during a disaster. Our analysis across different order quantities further supports this, showing that suppliers’ prosocial interest in speeding up delivery remains unaffected by procurement quantity. To delve deeper into the drivers of suppliers’ prosocial behavior, we employ an interaction effect analysis. This reveals that the buyer’s donation intent and crisis information act interchangeably in influencing suppliers’ decisions to deliver products faster and cheaper. This finding supports that both the buyer’s donation intent and crisis information effectively signal an urgent need. We then survey and interview B2B suppliers to further confirm the mechanisms of (i) the suppliers’ trust in the buyer’s donation intent and crisis information, (ii) awareness of need and moral elevation after knowing the buyer’s donation, and (iii) awareness of need after receiving the buyer’s crisis information. Supplementary interviews and analyses are conducted to either rule out alternative mechanisms, verify the robustness of our results, or both. We collaborated with the company to run a post-disaster field experiment. When PPE shortages ceased in the new normal, we found that suppliers still quoted faster delivery to donors than resellers. However, unlike the early crisis stages, this faster delivery does not come with a lower wholesale price. Suppliers are now hesitant to lower the wholesale price because of the associated costs, resulting in a null effect on the wholesale price decision. Similarly, providing COVID information of the buyer’s operating region to suppliers does not trigger an awareness of urgent need, failing to influence their lead time and wholesale price decisions. We also verify that the resulting differences between early- and post-disaster experiments are not due to the supplier difference between the two experiments.
article.2. Literature Review and Our Contributio: 2.1. Lead Time The delivery lead time is central to operations. The literature has analytically identified lead time as a key mechanism, allowing suppliers to quote varying lead times to buyers based on factors like buyers’ waiting costs (Liu et al. 2007) and suppliers’ capacity costs (Boyaci and Ray 2003). The empirical literature focuses on B2C markets, where the lead time (or product delivery time) as a consumer service impacts consumer demand (Cui et al. 2020), demand sensitivity (Lim et al. 2024), business performance (Fisher et al. 2019), and consumer behavior (Cohen et al. 2022). However, the literature lacks understanding of the B2B lead times, which significantly differ from those in B2C markets in terms of buyer type (reseller versus end consumer), buyer-seller relationship (long versus short term), seller decision (negotiable versus fixed), and buyer decision (rational versus emotional). To the best of our knowledge, the only empirical study on B2B lead time is byTerwiesch et al. (2005), who found that suppliers’ ability to meet the buyer’s requested delivery date (for semiconductor equipment) depends on their forecasting behavior. We follow up with an empirical study of suppliers’ lead time decisions, especially when the on-time arrival of PPE supplies is critical to saving the lives and livelihoods of frontline responders. In contrast to the standard view that faster product delivery is accompanied by a higher cost, we find that B2B buyers can receive products faster at a lower cost. 2.2. Corporate Prosocial Behavior “[E]mpirical research in behavioral economics has established that people generally demonstrate some prosocial preferences,” and consumers are accordingly attracted to firms that engage in key social issues (Singh et al. 2019). For example,Lev et al. (2010)andHainmueller et al. (2015)have shown that a company’s charitable giving behavior can effectively increase demand from some customer segments, thereby potentially increasing the company’s profits. In a recent influential study,Singh et al. (2019)conducted field experiments with a taxi-booking company, where consumers received either a discount-based promotion or a charity-linked promotion in which the company donated a specific amount of money to a charitable cause. They found little evidence that consumers benefitted from the company’s philanthropy; charity-linked promotion was less accepted than discount-based promotion and was less sensitive to the specific amount involved. This was consistent with the “warm-glow giving” theory, in which people get the most utility from the mere act of helping others, regardless of the social benefits associated with the helping behavior (Andreoni 1990,Singh et al. 2019). We differ from these studies by exploring how B2B suppliers respond to a buyer’s charitable giving behavior, particularly donations, thereby making two key contributions. First, we are the first to empirically study the effect of a buying company’s philanthropic activity on its upstream suppliers during the COVID-19 outbreak and the new normal. We find that suppliers deliver products to the donating buyer faster than to the reselling buyer. Through two B2B field experiments and a supplier survey, we identify that this is perhaps attributable to the suppliers’ prosocial behavior—the moral elevation and empathy-helping—during a disaster. Second, and interestingly, we find that the suppliers’ prosocial behavior is “pure” in that they distribute products to donors faster and at a cheaper price. This is different from the classical view that a firm’s socially responsible activities, such as philanthropic initiatives, complement traditional brand-building efforts, thereby (at least, potentially) benefiting the firm itself (Andreoni 1989,Hainmueller et al. 2015). Our study is particularly related to recent supply chain literature on social responsibility. This literature focuses on different mechanisms—such as sourcing contracts (Guo et al. 2016), mutual commitments (Chen et al. 2017), audit penalty (Caro et al. 2018), and disclosure mandates (Kalkanci and Plambeck 2020)—to induce the responsible behavior of B2B suppliers. We add to this literature by exploring two new mechanisms (donation and crisis information disclosure). We find that both mechanisms inspire suppliers’ prosocial behavior, but with nuanced differences. Specifically, a buyer’s donation intent signals the urgent needs of victims and the buyer’s helping behavior, whereas the crisis information makes the severity of the crisis informatively salient. 2.3. Disaster Management Various models have been developed to enable timely and effective responses to disasters.MacKenzie et al. (2014)developed a model of severe supply chain disruptions with alternative suppliers to characterize the optimal post-disaster decision making and apply the model to simulations based on the Japanese earthquake and tsunami.Zhang et al. (2022)proposed a dynamic relocation model using dynamic programming to proactively respond to the changing relief demand forecasts over time. Research has also provided empirical evidence on the impact of disaster on the supply chain.Barrot and Sauvagnat (2016)detected that suppliers (particularly specific suppliers) hit by natural disasters impose losses on their buyers.Boehm et al. (2019)learned that the U.S. output of Japanese affiliates dramatically declined after the Tōhoku earthquake.Carvalho et al. (2021)discovered that the supply chain disruption caused by the 2011 Great East Japan Earthquake (GEJE) resulted in a decline in Japan’s real Gross Domestic Product growth.Hendricks et al. (2020)found that the GEJE resulted in a shareholder value loss and that upstream and downstream supply chain propagation effects were significantly negative.Pan et al. (2020)established consumers’ precautionary stockpiling behavior prior to the onset of hurricane landfalls and found that this, in turn, impacted retail operations, namely, in-store product availability. However, empirical research on whether and how suppliers offer humanitarian assistance in response to social emergencies during disasters is limited (Besiou and Van Wassenhove 2020). 2.4. Wholesale Pricing The research has analytically shown that it is optimal for suppliers to charge different buyers different wholesale prices based on market power (Lariviere and Porteus 2001) and forecasting ability (Taylor and Xiao 2010). The empirical literature has also found that a supplier may charge retailers different wholesale prices based on, for example, buyer intermediation (Tunca and Zhu 2018), supplier-buyer relationships (Zhang et al. 2014), and buyers’ innate characteristics (Cui et al. 2021,2022). We advance this literature in three ways. First, we find that suppliers charge wholesale prices based on buyer type (donor versus reseller) and emergency information. Second, we conduct two B2B experiments during and after the disaster, and a comparison of these two experiments reveals that suppliers are more prosocial during the disaster; that is, suppliers lower their wholesale price to a donor amid the disaster but not after the disaster. Finally, we empirically study a standard practice in which suppliers set both wholesale prices and lead times, thereby complementing the relevant modeling literature (Liu et al. 2007).
article.3. Research Hypotheses: In our research context, the buying company communicates its purchasing intention to suppliers, specifying whether the products are intended for donation or resale. Standard economic theory suggests that suppliers should discard the “cheap talk” about the buyer’s intention because it is impossible for suppliers to validate that information. However, in B2B environments characterized by enduring relationships, trust, trustworthiness, and commitment prevail between buyers and suppliers. Consequently, suppliers still consider the buyer’s intention information when deciding lead time quotations. Surveys and interviews corroborate this, affirming that suppliers trust the buyer’s donation information and charitable behavior (Section 6). According to social utility theory, individuals exhibit a willingness to reward acts of kindness, such as donations, a concept known as indirect reciprocity (Milinski et al. 2002.Khadjavi 2017). In this context, others’ donations can stimulatemoral elevation—the positive emotions induced by observing others’ virtuous or praiseworthy behavior (Haidt 2003). This sense of moral elevation subsequently motivates observers to engage in tangible altruistic and prosocial actions, seeking psychological benefits (Schnall et al. 2010). A buyer’s donation intent also signals that products are urgently needed. Intuitively, compared with commercial products, those donated in kind, such as PPE products, are used mainly for victims in urgent need amid a disaster; our aforementioned survey confirms this intuition. Considering the buyer’s urgency (i.e., low willingness to wait), suppliers would provide faster delivery to meet these pressing needs (Özer 2003,Allon and Bassamboo 2011); that is, the lower the end user’s willingness to wait, the shorter the lead time offered by the suppliers. Furthermore, the urgent need for donated products emphasizes the dire situation faced by victims, thereby making suppliers more empathetic to victims and increasing their willingness to help them, which is known as empathy helping. Taken together, both economic trade-off and social utility theory suggest that the buyer’s intent to donate—signaling his or her charitable giving behavior and the urgent need of victims—would lead to faster product delivery. (Donation).During disasters, the buyer’s intent to donate supplies can reduce the lead time quoted by suppliers. The buyer receives a quote with faster delivery when sourcing for donation than for resale. Presenting crisis information to suppliers, like the number of victims in the buyer’s region and the severity of the crisis relative to other regions, directly signals the urgent need of products. In fact, the awareness of need—knowledge and awareness of victims’ or beneficiaries’ needs and circumstances—plays a key role in driving individuals’ empathy-helping behavior (Bekkers and Wiepking 2011). Crisis information indicates a high level of severity of the disaster-stricken region, which is a more direct and explicit signal of the urgent need for products. Moreover, this specific crisis information would be effective, owing to its visceral and emotional content (Ryzhov et al. 2016), and thus plays a major role in convincing people to contribute amid disasters (Bennett and Kottasz 2000). Accordingly, after receiving the crisis information, suppliers would perceive the issue’s salience and become more empathetic, leading to greater altruistic intentions to help victims with faster delivery. In summary, as inHypothesis 1, the urgent need indicated by crisis information would stimulate suppliers to provide faster delivery by evoking their economic thinking on the end users’ low willingness-to-wait and empathy-helping behavior to help victims. (Crisis Information).During disasters, crisis information indicating a high level of severity can reduce the lead time quoted by suppliers. The buyer receives a quote with faster delivery when disclosing crisis information. Crisis information and donation intent can act as substitutes in shaping suppliers’ lead time decisions because both signals convey the urgent need for PPE products. Conversely, they can also function as complements; for instance, crisis information may accentuate the lead time reduction prompted by donation intent (moral elevation). Thus, we posit the following hypothesis. (Interaction Effect).Crisis information (donation) can(a)mitigate or(b)accentuate the lead time reduction associated with donation (crisis information). When deciding on a wholesale price, suppliers factor in the buyer’s intention and crisis information, which signals the urgent need of end users. The economic theory suggests that the supplier would quote a higher wholesale price to impatient buyers to take advantage of their urgent needs; see Proposition 1 in Online Appendix C for a theoretical illustration. Rather, a foresighted supplier might quote a lower wholesale price to the needy buyer because it can backfire economically if the supplier increases the price in a crisis or for donation orders. Moreover, as per social utility theory, the buyer’s intent to donate or crisis information would also stimulate the supplier to provide a lower wholesale price. Consequently, the ultimate impact depends on how suppliers balance their economic utility and social utility. (Wholesale Price).During disasters, the buyer’s intent to donate (crisis information) supplies can(a)increase or(b)reduce the wholesale price quoted by suppliers. Figure A.1 in Online Appendix A summarizes the effect of donation and crisis information. First, the buyer’s donation intent indicates the victims’ urgent need and the buyer’s charitable giving, thereby evoking suppliers’ moral elevation and empathy-helping intentions (social utility theory) and signaling a lower willingness to wait of victims (economic theory). Consequently,Hypothesis 1states that donation leads to a faster lead time. Second, the crisis information emphasizes the victims’ urgent need, thereby evoking suppliers’ empathy-helping intentions (social utility theory) and signaling a low willingness to wait of victims (economic theory). Consequently, the crisis information leads to a faster lead time, as described inHypothesis 2, and crisis information is substituted or reinforced with the donation in shaping the supplier’s lead time, as described inHypothesis 3. Third, the social utility theory predicts that the buyer’s donation intent or crisis information would lead to a lower wholesale price, whereas economic theory has no clear prediction. Consequently,Hypothesis 4has no clear directional prediction regarding the effect of donation and crisis information on wholesale price.
article.4. B2B Experiment Amid the COVID-19 Outb: We conducted a field experiment in collaboration with a global trading company. The company operates in the state of New Jersey, which had the second highest number of COVID-19 cases in the United States during April and May 2020. The company’s neighborhood faced a severe shortage of PPE during the early stages of the COVID-19 outbreak. In response to this need, the trading company started to source PPE products from China and then either sold or directly donated them to local communities in urgent need.3 The trading company purchased products through Alibaba. Suppliers who sell on Alibaba provide company information on a profile page and list product information on a product page. The profile page displays basic company information, such as name, location, and size, and trading performance, such as response rate, ratings, and number of transactions. Suppliers can pay to become gold suppliers and have a premium membership that confers advantages in terms of product promotion and exposure. The product page displays product characteristics (e.g., description, picture, and price) and transaction details (e.g., customization and payment method). Please see Figure A.2 in Online Appendix A for a sample product page. A buyer can search for a specific product and then view different supplier profiles and product details, as depicted in Figure A.2. The buyer can then choose a supplier and, if desired, click the “Contact Supplier” button to send inquiries to negotiate price and other transaction details, such as the lead time and shipping method. After receiving a buyer’s inquiry, the supplier can choose whether to follow up. After the transaction details, such as the wholesale price and lead time, are agreed upon, the buyer makes a payment, the supplier ships the order, and the transaction is completed. 4.1. Study Design The trading company selected a sample of 4,040 products from 4,040 suppliers. The products were similar in quality and included face masks, safety gloves, safety goggles, and safety clothes, which are among the most widely used standard PPE products. The trading company specified the product categories from which we independently selected suppliers and products.Table 1summarizes our2×2×22×2×2experimental design. Suppliers were randomly assigned to one of eight treatment arms. We assigned 2,020 suppliers per sourcing purpose, 2,020 suppliers per information condition, 2,020 suppliers per order quantity, and 505 suppliers per treatment arm. This means that each supplier quoted only once. To avoid possible confounders related to procurement accounts or representatives, the company used only one procurement account to send inquiries to approximately 20 suppliers for each treatment—that is, approximately 160 (20 × 8) suppliers—every day and finally completed the inquiry process to all 4,040 suppliers from April 18, 2020, to May 12, 2020. Table 1.Field Experiment Design Table 1.Field Experiment Design Notes.“Low” and “High” indicate low quantity and high quantity, respectively. The planned sample size was 4,040, that is, 505 suppliers per treatment arm. The actual sample size was 2,771 after excluding unavailable listings. We conducted a randomization check across the following supplier features: (1) gold supplier status (i.e., the duration (in years) that the supplier had been an elite member on Alibaba), (2) response rate over the last 30 days, (3) transaction level (i.e., the cumulative score based on the transaction volume on Alibaba), (4) the number of transactions over the last six months, (5) the number of reviews, (6) review ratings, and (7) the listed price of the selected product. Table A.1 in Online Appendix A shows the summary statistics of these variables. As can be seen, thep-values in Table A.2 are all larger than 0.05, which ensures randomization. We also ensure that the listed prices for 5,000 units (“low quantity”) and 50,000 units (“high quantity”) are not statistically different (p-value = 0.66). 4.2. Study Procedure The trading company sent inquiries to the selected suppliers during a period of severe PPE shortage. Each inquiry asked for a lead time quote and a wholesale price quote for either 5,000 or 50,000 units of the preselected product. These order quantities follow the industry practices and norms of global trading, that is, not to quote small quantities, such as 1,000 or fewer units, or large quantities, such as 100,000 or more units, in the first inquiry. The message content varied in terms of the sourcing purposes and information conditions. The inquiry messages are presented in Figure A.3. Under the “no information” condition, we have two buyer purchasing scenarios. The first is the standard resale case. The buyer sends an inquiry message with the most basic information and has no need to pinpoint his or her purpose: “Hello, we are interested in this product. [Alibaba provides the product name and webpage]. Could you please quote us (1) your best price per piece (exw) for an order of [5,000 or 50,000] units (2) and your earliest time of delivery (lead time)? Thanks.” Here, exw or “ExWorks” is an international trade term meaning that a supplier provides products at a designated location in the supplier’s country, and the buyer (not the supplier) bears the transportation costs. It is an industry norm to use the exw term when making an initial quotation, and it has also been adopted in previous B2B field studies (Cui et al. 2021). The second scenario is the donation case, where the buyer states, “We will donate this order ([5,000 or 50,000] units) to the local community.” The industry norm is to ask both the lead time and wholesale price in the first inquiry. During a crisis, global transportation may be affected by many uncontrollable factors; thus, we consider the lead time as the time between the order being placed and the order being ready for delivery, which is not confounded by transportation distances or other transportation factors. Under the “crisis information” condition, the buyer reveals that he or she operates in a virus-stricken region: “We are located in New Jersey, which has the second most COVID-19 cases (more than [80,000 from April 18 to April 23, 2020, and 100,000 from April 24 to May 12, 2020]) in the US.” The inquiry message reports the number of confirmed cases in a region, which is the norm in disclosing COVID-19 information by organizations such as the World Health Organization.4 Within a week after the inquiry, we recorded and compared the lead times and wholesale prices in the initial quotes. Of the 4,040 products, only 2,771 were available, mainly because Alibaba and the Chinese government started to impose strict quality control on exporting PPE amid the COVID-19 outbreak. If the supplier provided a quote, the buyer replied, “Thank you for your kind reply.” We obtained 494 responses, corresponding to a 17.8% response rate. The response rate is likely low because of the significant changes in market dynamics, such as surging demand, emerging PPE fraud, and transportation disruption, and because some suppliers did not meet export requirements, such as Food and Drug Administration certification. Nonetheless, our response rate is fairly high compared with the response rates (often approximately 1%) in B2C environments, and the resulting sample size is comparable to other B2B field studies (Craig et al. 2016,Simester 2017).
article.5. Estimation Results: We compare the observed lead time and wholesale price quotations across different treatment arms on the sourcing purpose (donation versus resell) and information conditions (“crisis information” versus “no information”). 5.1. Lead Time 5.1.1. Donation. Table A.3 in Online Appendix A andFigure 1show that buyers informing suppliers that the PPE products are used for donation receive a quote with a significantly faster lead time (in days) than buyers who are reselling (p-value = 0.00). We also formally examine this: wherePurposeiis a binary variable that represents the purpose (resale or donation) in quotei, andControlsiis a vector of control variables regarding supplier characteristics, including gold supplier status, the response rate, transaction level, number of reviews, review rating, and listed price. The estimation results are presented in column I of panel A inTable 2, where the omitted buyers’ purpose is resale. The coefficient of Donation in the table represents the additional lead time offered to donors relative to resellers, which is significantly negative (p-value < 0.01). This implies that suppliers quote a significantly lower lead time to the donor than to the reseller, thereby supportingHypothesis 1. Table 2.Effect of Donation and Crisis Information Table 2.Effect of Donation and Crisis Information Notes.This table reports the estimated coefficients and robust standard errors (in parentheses) for the effect of donation or crisis information on lead time and wholesale price. Columns I–IV are based on the full sample. **𝑝<0.05p<0.05; ***𝑝<0.01p<0.01. 5.1.2. Crisis Information. Table A.3 in Online Appendix A andFigure 1show that crisis information significantly reduces the lead time quoted to buyers (p-value = 0.00). We formally examine this: 5.1.3. Relative Effect. We investigate the moderating effect of order quantity: 5.2. Wholesale Price We calculate the markup percentage of the quoted price relative to the listed price because the COVID-19 outbreak pushed up suppliers’ quoted prices for PPE compared with the listed price: Table A.4 summarizes the suppliers’ markups for buyers who are reselling, those who are donating, and those under the “no information” and “crisis information” conditions; see the visual representation inFigure 2. We also formally test the markup difference based on the buyers’ purpose: Column III of panels A and B inTable 2presents the estimation results; the coefficient of Donation represents the additional markup for buyers who source products for donation compared with buyers who source products for resale, whereas the coefficient of Crisis Information represents the additional markup for buyers under the “crisis information” condition compared with buyers under the “no information” condition. We make two key observations. First,Figure 2and Table A.4 show that donation intent significantly reduces the wholesale price quoted to buyers (p-value​=0.03​=0.03). Moreover, column III of panel A inTable 2shows that the coefficient of Donation is significantly negative (p-value​<0.05​<0.05). This indicates that in line with the results of lead time, donation also reduces the wholesale price quoted to buyers. Second,Figure 2and Table A.4 show that crisis information significantly reduces the wholesale price quoted to buyers (p-value​=0.03​=0.03). Column III of panel B inTable 2also shows that crisis information reduces the wholesale price quoted to buyers (p-value​<0.05​<0.05), thereby supportingHypothesis 4(b). Given these two observations, we conjecture that suppliers’ social utility dominates their economic thinking because otherwise they would price higher, or at least similarly,5by taking advantage of the buyer’s low willingness to wait (which is signaled by his or her donation intent and crisis information). This is also consistent with the disaster-as-galvanizer hypothesis that companies are more likely to empathize with and want to take on the “social responsibility” to help others recover as quickly as possible amid disasters (Muller and Kräussl 2011). That is, companies act more pro-socially and altruistically during a disaster than during normal times; seeSection 8for more evidence. Finally, we examine the moderating effect of order quantity on wholesale price reduction: Column IV ofTable 2presents the estimation results, in which the coefficients of Donation × High Quantity and Crisis Information × High Quantity represent the moderating effect of high-order quantity on wholesale price reduction associated with donation intent and crisis information, respectively. This indicates that the coefficients of Donation × High Quantity and Crisis Information × High Quantity are not significant; increasing order quantity has no significant influence on the wholesale price reduction associated with donation intent and sharing crisis information. Similar to the results of lead time inSection 5.1, wholesale price reduction associated with donation intent or crisis information is also not affected by the order quantity. Different from the lead time reduction, the wholesale price reduction means a monetary loss for suppliers, especially when the order quantity is relatively high. For insight, recall that suppliers’ prosocial utility dominates their economic thinking amid a disaster. This implies that suppliers derive the most psychological benefits from rewarding buyers, regardless of how many products are donated or delivered to the crisis-affected region, owing to the warm-glow effect.
article.6. Mechanism: To further validate the mechanism behind suppliers’ prosocial behavior, we (i) explore the interaction effect between donation intent and crisis information, (ii) survey and interview suppliers, and (iii) exclude other possible mechanisms. 6.1. Interaction Effect Between Donation and Crisis Information We test the interaction effect of crisis information and donation intent: 6.2. Supplier Survey and Interview We surveyed 57 professionals with an average of 6.7 years of working experience in global trading. They were not involved in our experiment so that their answers would not be confounded with their experimental experience. We asked them to measure how much they agreed with the following (see Figure D.1 in Online Appendix D): (1) they trust the donation information (“we will donate this order to the local community”) and crisis information (“we are located in New Jersey, which has the second most COVID-19 cases in the U.S.”); (2) they are aware of the buyer’s urgent need because of his or her donation information; (3) they are willing to help the buyer because they feel that the buyer’s need is very urgent from the donation information; (4) they are willing to help the buyer because they are inspired by the buyer’s donation to reward his or her kindness; (5) they are aware of the buyer’s urgent need because of the buyer’s crisis information; (6) they are willing to help the buyer because they feel that the buyer’s need is very urgent from the crisis information; and (7) as long as they help others, they are satisfied, and they rarely consider how much help they have provided. The level of agreement is measured on a seven-point semantic differential scale from 1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree). Table D.1 in Online Appendix D presents the results. Regarding (1), suppliers trust the donation information (trust score​=5.39​=5.39, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000) and the crisis information (trust score​=5.12​=5.12, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000). Regarding (2–4), suppliers are aware of the buyer’s urgent need from his or her donation behavior (agreement level​=5.51​=5.51, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000) and consequently are willing to help the buyer (agreement level​=5.88​=5.88, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000). In addition, suppliers are willing to reward the buyer’s kindness (agreement level​=5.95​=5.95, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000). Regarding (5–6), suppliers are aware of the buyer’s urgent need from his or her crisis information (agreement level​=5.35​=5.35, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000). Consequently, suppliers are willing to help the buyer (agreement level​=5.72​=5.72, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000). Regarding (7), as long as suppliers help others, they are satisfied, and they rarely consider how much help they have provided (agreement level​=4.67​=4.67, significantly higher than the neutral point,p-value​=0.000​=0.000). In summary, our survey confirms the suppliers’ behavior: (i) trust in the buyer’s donation and crisis information, (ii) awareness of the urgent need and moral elevation after knowing the buyer’s donation behavior, (iii) awareness of the urgent need after receiving the buyer’s crisis information, and (iv) warm-glow giving. We also interview suppliers who were the samples in our experiment. Their interview quotes are summarized in Table D.2 in Online Appendix D. Their quotes are consistent with our survey, thereby again supporting the aforementioned behavior (i)–(iv). 6.3. Guilt of Suppliers The buyer’s donation behavior and crisis information might invoke the guilty feeling of Chinese suppliers, especially those in close proximity to Wuhan. This, in turn, could motivate suppliers to reduce their lead time and wholesale price. To test this, we collect the supplier information—(i) whether the supplier is located in China and (ii) the Chinese supplier’s distance to Wuhan—to run the following: Moreover, we rerunEquations (1)–(6) but incorporateChineseiandDistancei, respectively, as control; the estimation results are shown in Tables A.7 and A.8, respectively, in Online Appendix A. Again, we find our results to be robust: (i) donation or crisis information significantly reduces the lead time and wholesale price quoted by the suppliers; (ii) order quantity does not affect the lead time reduction and wholesale price reduction associated with donation or crisis information; and (iii) donation and crisis information are substitutes for reducing the lead time and wholesale price. To better understand these results, we surveyed 55 Chinese suppliers with an average of 6.9 years of working experience in global trading. We asked them to indicate how much they agreed on the following. (1) They are willing to help the buyer because they feel guilty after seeing the donation information, and (2) they are willing to help the buyer because they feel guilty after seeing the crisis information. Table D.3 in Online Appendix D presents the results; the suppliers do not feel guilty after receiving the donation information (agreement level​=4.04​=4.04,p-value​=0.859​=0.859) and crisis information (agreement level​=4.27​=4.27,p-value​=0.235​=0.235). In summary, the Chinese suppliers’ helping behavior for donor or reseller with crisis information is unlikely because they feel guilty.
article.7. Robustness Check: We conduct additional analyses to check the robustness of our main results with respect to the supplier selection and the B2B trading features (the time and product, inventory, transportation cost, on-time fulfillment rate, and order quantity). 7.1. Sample Selection Bias The supplier’s decision on whether to respond to the buyer’s inquiry is not necessarily exogenous. To rule out the selection bias, we conduct three attrition tests in Online Appendix B, which suggest that suppliers randomly enter our sample. We then use a Heckman two-step method (Heckman 1979). In the first step, we estimate the probability of an observation entering a sample: Using the estimation results of binary logistic regression (8), we construct inverse Mills ratios (IMR) and incorporate these ratios into the second-stage regressions. In the second step, we use the following equation to estimate the effect of donation intent and crisis information on the suppliers’ quoted price and lead time after correcting the selected samples: 7.2. Time Fixed Effect and Product Type Effect Different suppliers might take different times to respond to buyer inquiries; therefore, the quotation dates for inquiries sent out on the same day may vary. We thus incorporate the time fixed effects at two levels: the inquires’ request date and inquires’ quote date. We also classify PPE products into four types: face masks, safety gloves, safety goggles, and safety clothes. We performed a balance check and found no significant difference in product type between the treatment and control groups (p-value > 0.1). We reranEquations (1)–(6) by incorporating the product type as a control variable and including the request time fixed effects (or quote time fixed effects).6The estimation results are shown in Tables A.10 and A.11 in Online Appendix A. We have four key observations. First, the coefficient of Donation in column I of panel A in Tables A.10 and A.11 is significantly negative (p-value​<0.01​<0.01), confirming that buyers’ intention to donate can speed up the delivery. Second, the coefficient of Crisis Information in column I of panel B in Tables A.10 and A.11 is significantly negative (p-value​<0.01​<0.01), which again confirms that presenting crisis information can reduce the lead time. Moreover, the coefficient of Donation × Crisis Information in panel C of Tables A.10 and A.11 is significantly positive (p-value​<0.05​<0.05), thereby confirmingHypothesis 3(a). Third, the coefficient of Donation in column III of panel A in Tables A.10 and A.11 is significantly negative (p-value​<0.01​<0.01), thereby supporting that donation reduces the wholesale price. Moreover, the coefficient of Crisis Information in column III of panel B in Tables A.10 and A.11 is significantly negative (p-value​<0.05​<0.05), thereby supporting that crisis information reduces the wholesale price. Fourth, the coefficients of Donation × High Quantity and Crisis Information × High Quantity in columns II and IV of Tables A.10 and A.11 are statistically insignificant, which again confirms that increasing order quantity has no significant influence on the lead time reduction and wholesale price reduction associated with donation intent or crisis information. 7.3. Inventory Availability The suppliers’ reply messages may contain information regarding their inventory availability. Some suppliers might reply with something like “we don’t have it in stock” or quote a long lead time, thereby implying no inventory readily available. We accordingly match these suppliers to the most “similar” nonrespondent suppliers in terms of all the supplier characteristics via a propensity score matching (PSM). In this manner, we construct an indicator variable for each supplier to represent their inventory availability. First, we conduct a randomization check and find no significant differences on the inventory availability between our control and treatment groups (p-value > 0.05). Second, we use this inventory availability variable as a control to rerunEquations (1)–(6) and find consistent results (Table A.12).7Third, we use this variable as a moderator and find that it does not affect the lead time reduction and wholesale price reduction associated with donation or crisis information (Table A.13). 7.4. Transportation Cost In our experiment, the analysis is a specific exw term where suppliers are not responsible for the order shipment. We expected our results to continue to hold for the situation in which suppliers assume the order shipment, because the key consideration for buyers and suppliers during a disaster was the lead time, rather than transportation, which is usually not costly for PPE products. To further confirm this, we calculated the geographic distance between each supplier and the buyer, which can be a proxy of the transportation cost. Accordingly, we conducted three analyses. First, we conducted a balance check and found that suppliers in control and treatment groups had similar distances to the buying company (p-value > 0.1). Second, we reranEquations (1)–(6) but incorporated the supplier-buyer distance as a control variable. The estimation results are shown in Table A.14, where the results are consistent with our main results. Third, we used the supplier-buyer distance as a moderator and found that the distance did not affect the lead time reduction and wholesale price reduction associated with donation or crisis information (Table A.15). 7.5. On-time Fulfillment Rate The on-time fulfillment rate (i.e., the percentage of orders shipped within the expected time frame) is a key performance metric for suppliers. To check whether our results were contingent on it, we procured the data on each supplier’s on-time fulfillment rate, and accordingly had three findings. First, we found no significant differences on the on-time fulfillment rate between our control and treatment groups (p-value > 0.1). Second, we tested whether the on-time fulfillment rate affected our main results by rerunningEquations (1)–(6) but incorporating it as a control variable. The estimation results are shown in Table A.16, where the results are consistent with our main results. Third, we tested the moderating effect and found that the on-time fulfillment rate did not affect the lead time reduction and wholesale price reduction associated with donation or crisis information (Table A.17). 7.6. Subsample with Order Quantity We rerunEquations (1), (2), (4), and (6) under different quantity conditions (“high quantity” versus “low quantity”) and present the estimation results in Table A.18. We also present the summary statistics of lead times and markups under different quantities for resale and donation or for no information and crisis information in Tables A.3 and A.4; see the visual representation in Figure A.4. We have three key observations. First, the results on lead time inSection 5.1remain robust, both visually and statistically. Specifically, the formal regression shows that suppliers significantly shorten the lead time for donating buyers, regardless of the order quantity; see columns I–II of panel A in Table A.18. This is consistent with the visual presentation in Figure A.4(a) and the summary statistics in Table A.3, where donation significantly reduces the lead time for buyers under both the “low-quantity” condition (p-value​=0.01​=0.01int-test) and the “high-quantity” condition (p-value​=0.05​=0.05int-test). Moreover, the formal regression shows that crisis information significantly reduces the lead time regardless of the order quantity; see columns I and II of panel B in Table A.18. This is consistent with Figure A.4(b) and Table A.3 withp-value​=0.05​=0.05andp-value​=0.03​=0.03under the “low-quantity” condition and the “high-quantity” condition, respectively. Second, the results on wholesale price inSection 5.2are visually robust in Figure A.4(c–d). Rather, as shown in columns III–IV of panels A and B in Table A.18, the formal regression yields two insignificant wholesale price results: (i) the impact of donation under the “low-quantity” condition and (ii) the impact of crisis information under the “high-quantity” condition.8That is, the price is not significantly cheaper under certain order quantity conditions. This insignificance is owing to perhaps the limited sample size of subsample. Third, crisis information could mitigate the lead time reduction and wholesale price reduction associated with donation; see panel C of Table A.18.
article.8. A Post-Disaster Experiment: To check whether our key findings during the COVID-19 outbreak still hold when the disaster ends, we conducted a field experiment in the new normal period (August 20, 2022, to September 10, 2022). The collaborating company repeats the2×2×22×2×2experiment inSection 4to test suppliers’ responses to a buyer’s lead time and wholesale price inquiries for two sourcing purposes (resale versus donation) under two information conditions (with and without COVID information). Although the COVID information cannot signal the emergency level of buyers in the new normal period, we still keep the COVID information treatment for the sake of consistency. This also helps us disentangle the pure impact of presenting COVID information and the pure impact of signaling emergency. We selected a new sample of 4,040 products from 4,040 suppliers, which were not included in the sample of our main experiment inSection 4. This new pool of suppliers has two advantages. First, it helps avoid possible confounding factors caused by quotations during the COVID outbreak. For example, if suppliers ever received a quotation with donation and crisis information, they might change their perception about such information and consequently alter their quotation decisions. Second, this helps us obtain a reasonable sample size because many suppliers during the COVID-19 outbreak were not available in the post-pandemic PPE market. Again, we conducted a randomization check to ensure that the suppliers were randomly assigned (Table A.20). Table A.19 shows the summary statistics of the supplier characteristic variables. The collaborating company tailored the messages to incorporate different purposes, information conditions, and order quantities. The messages under the “no information” condition remained unchanged—that is, (i) for a standard resale purpose, the company sent: “Hello, we are interested in this product. [Alibaba provides the product name and webpage]. Could you please quote us (1) your best price per piece (exw) for an order of [5,000 or 50,000] units and (2) your earliest time of delivery (lead time)? Thanks.” and (ii) to indicate a donation purpose, we add “We will donate this order ([5,000 or 50,000] units) to the local community.” Under the “COVID-related information” condition, the message changed by revealing the buyer’s COVID information in the post-disaster phase: “We are located in New Jersey, which has more than 3,000 COVID-19 cases every day.” The trading company used a procurement representative account to send inquiries to suppliers on Alibaba. Within a week after the inquiry, we recorded and compared the lead time and wholesale price quotes. Owing to various factors, such as the increasing standard for exporting PPE, we received 251 responses from 1,162 available suppliers, corresponding to a 21.6% response rate. This is higher than that during the COVID-19 outbreak (17.8%) because perhaps the B2B business processes had reverted to more normal levels. We also conducted the Heckman two-step approach to exclude possible selection bias effect; see Table A.23 in Online Appendix A for the estimation results, which show that our results still hold. 8.1. Lead Time We runEquations (1)–(3), using the post-disaster experimental data, and then present the estimation results in panels A and B in Table A.22. In addition, Table A.21 presents the summary statistics for the lead time with donation (no information) and resale (COVID information) in the post-disaster phase; seeFigure 3for a visual illustration. We find that donation still significantly reduces the lead time. In particular, the coefficient of Donation is significantly negative in column I of panel A in Table A.22 (p-value​<0.01​<0.01). This is consistent with Table A.21 andFigure 3, indicating that suppliers quote an average lead time of 12.92 days to buyers that are reselling and 9.41 days to buyers that are donating (p-value​=0.01​=0.01). Recall that a buyer’s intention to donate can reduce the lead time at the early stages of a disaster because the donation information signals both the urgent need of victims and the buyer’s charitable giving behavior to suppliers, as shown inSection 3. In the new normal, when PPE products are no longer urgently needed, a buyer’s intent to donate would signal only the buyer’s charitable giving behavior to the suppliers, and the suppliers would still pro-socially reduce their lead time to reward the buyer’s kindness. Moreover, increasing order quantity would not affect lead time reduction; the coefficient of Donation × High Quantity is not statistically significant. This is because lead time reduction incurs little monetary loss for the suppliers, and the warm-glow effect suggests that suppliers derive the most psychological benefits from rewarding the buyers with faster delivery, regardless of the order quantities. However, COVID-related information has no significant impact on lead time. That is, when products are no longer urgently needed, presenting COVID-related information fails to evoke suppliers’ awareness of need and empathy-helping intentions and thus cannot inspire their prosocial behavior. Thus, we can fully attribute the results inSection 5on the impact of crisis information to the emergency information contained in the crisis information. 8.2. Wholesale Price After the pandemic, it is common practice for suppliers to privately quote a lower price than their publicly listed prices. Thus, we calculate the discount: We repeat the same analyses specified inEquations (4)and (5), and the estimation results are shown in panels A and B in Table A.22. In addition, Table A.21 presents the summary statistics for the wholesale price with donation (no information) and resale (COVID information) in the post-disaster phase.Figure 4presents a visual illustration. We find that donation has no significant influence on the suppliers’ wholesale price because the coefficient of Donation in column III of panel A in Table A.22 is insignificant. This is consistent with Table A.21 andFigure 4. Recall that during a disaster, a buyer’s intent to donate leads to a lower wholesale price because suppliers’ social utility to lower the wholesale price dominates their economic benefit of a higher wholesale price (Section 3). However, without the PPE shortage, on the one hand, the buyer’s donation does not signal the urgent need for products. On the other hand, the suppliers start to consider the economic losses of lowering the wholesale price. Eventually, donation does not change the suppliers’ wholesale price. Similarly, COVID information does not affect suppliers’ wholesale prices, as shown in column III of panel B in Table A.22, where the coefficient of COVID information is insignificant. This is consistent with Table A.21 andFigure 4. Recall that presenting crisis information can reduce the wholesale price at the early stages of a disaster (Section 3). In the post-disaster phase, where PPE products are no longer an urgent need, presenting COVID information fails to evoke the suppliers’ awareness of an urgent need or empathy-helping intentions, thereby not affecting their wholesale price decisions. 8.3. Interaction Effect We rerunEquation (6)and present estimation results in panel C of Table A.22. The coefficient of Donation × COVID information is insignificant; COVID information does not affect the lead time reduction associated with donation because donation helps buyers receive faster delivery, whereas presenting COVID information fails to do so.
article.9. Implications and Conclusion: We conducted a unique B2B field experiment in the early stages of the COVID-19 outbreak and found that when a buyer purchased products for donation or presented crisis information to his or her suppliers, the suppliers provided faster delivery at a cheaper price. We then repeated field experiments after the disaster and found that the buyer’s donation purpose reduced lead time, even when the products were no longer urgently needed. However, providing COVID-related information to suppliers failed to impact the suppliers’ lead time and wholesale price quotes. 9.1. Modeling Implications Our empirical results provide evidence on how suppliers quote lead time and wholesale price to their buyers during and after a disaster. One might argue (i) how the suppliers’ prosocial behavior would theoretically affect their lead time and wholesale price decisions, (ii) whether the theoretical results are consistent with the empirical results, and (iii) how the suppliers characterize their lead time and wholesale price decisions with different settings. We then incorporate our findings into a supply chain model consisting of one supplier and one retailer, where the retailer collects market information, such as the quantity of demand, and then sends an inquiry to the supplier (Online Appendix C). The supplier quotes the lead time and wholesale price to the retailer. Then, the reselling retailer decides on retail price, and the donating retailer donates the product to the end users. The prediction of the model is consistent with our hypothesis development inSection 3. For example, the supplier’s equilibrium lead time increases in the end user’s willingness to wait, and the equilibrium wholesale price increases in the end user’s willingness to pay (Proposition 1). We also find that the supplier’s prosocial behavior (particularly, moral elevation and empathy-helping) lowers the wholesale price and lead time quotes (Propositions 2 and 3). Moreover, our model can help suppliers characterize their lead time and wholesale price decisions in different settings and can be extended to different settings to incorporate different market factors, such as customer segmentation. 9.2. Managerial Implications 9.2.1. Disaster Response. During disasters, companies often respond by acquiring and donating products to those affected. Our research indicates that, apart from enhancing customer goodwill and bolstering brand reputation for post-disaster sales, such donations empower companies to acquire products at a reduced cost and facilitate their swift distribution to victims. If the company chooses to donate, our findings suggest keeping donations modest for profit maximization because suppliers gain psychological benefits from rewarding buyers regardless of the quantity donated. Alternatively, if the company opts to resell, disclosing urgent needs to suppliers can prompt them to prioritize orders. In making these decisions, the company should also factor in, for example, inventory storage and ordering costs paid to the suppliers. For a government agency aiming to improve disaster response efficiency, our results suggest that it should make crisis information (e.g., number of victims) publicly available and accessible to suppliers. This would help buying companies and victims quickly receive the necessary products. Moreover, the government agency should encourage purchasing companies to donate products, which could speed up product delivery. Finally, the government agency should explore collaboration with charitable organizations for product procurement, given that their charitable nature would help inspire prosocial behavior in other parties (e.g., suppliers). 9.2.2. Normal Operations. For a sourcing company, our findings suggest that it should choose to donate to expedite delivery because its purpose of donation evokes suppliers’ prosocial behavior, thereby allowing it to get delivery faster. Therefore, signaling the donation purpose to the suppliers would be effective for a company seeking quicker delivery. For a government agency, our work indicates that it should encourage philanthropic actions of sourcing companies, which can help evoke suppliers’ prosocial behavior and improve supply chain delivery efficiency. The transmission of such philanthropic actions among B2B companies is conducive to improving supply chain efficiency, enhancing social welfare, and building a harmonious society.
